items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Cognitive dissonance is the mental discomfort we feel when our actions and our beliefs don't line up. Psychologist Leon Festinger introduced the term in the 1950s, and it remains one of the most influential ideas in social psychology. The basic claim is simple: when we hold two ideas that clash, or when we behave in a way that contradicts what we believe, we feel an unpleasant tension. And because that tension is uncomfortable, we're motivated to reduce it. Now, here's the interesting part. Often the easiest way to reduce the discomfort isn't to change our behavior. It's to change our attitude. Let me give you the classic study. Festinger and his colleague paid participants to tell another person that a boring task was actually fun. Some were paid twenty dollars to lie, others just one dollar. You might expect the people paid more to like the task more. But the opposite happened. The people paid only one dollar later rated the boring task as genuinely enjoyable. Why? The twenty-dollar group had a solid external reason for lying, so they felt no tension. The one-dollar group had no good excuse, so to resolve the discomfort, they convinced themselves the task really was interesting. Their attitude shifted to match their behavior. This tells us something important about how attitudes form. We tend to assume our behavior flows from our beliefs. But dissonance theory flips that around. Sometimes our beliefs quietly rearrange themselves to justify what we've already done. So attitude change isn't always a careful, rational process. Often it's the mind working to keep us feeling consistent with ourselves.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How cognitive dissonance can drive a change in attitude
options.B: The amount of money people will accept to lie
options.C: How rewards reliably make boring tasks feel enjoyable
options.D: The career and major writings of Leon Festinger

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整篇讲座的核心是认知失调如何促使人改变态度来缓解不适，Festinger的实验正是为说明这一点。B是too_narrow，只抓住实验中付钱说谎的细节，不是主旨。C是reversed_logic/wrong_topic，讲座恰恰说明付钱少的人才更喜欢任务，奖励并不可靠地让任务变有趣。D是wrong_topic，讲座只提到Festinger提出该理论，并未讲他的生平著作。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the experiment with the one-dollar and twenty-dollar payments?

options.A: To show that higher pay always produces more honest behavior
options.B: To illustrate how people change attitudes to reduce dissonance
options.C: To prove that the boring task was actually enjoyable
options.D: To argue that money is the strongest motivator of belief

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授举付一美元和二十美元的实验，目的是具体展示人们如何通过改变态度来缓解失调——一美元组没有外部借口，便说服自己任务有趣。A是wrong_purpose，曲解为讨论诚实，与目的无关。C是literal_restatement/wrong_purpose，任务本身是无聊的，被试只是改变了看法。D是over_inference，教授并未论证金钱是信念最强的动机，反而说明钱多反而没改变态度。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that dissonance theory 'flips that around'?

options.A: To restate that the experiment used two payment groups
options.B: To suggest that Festinger disproved all earlier psychology
options.C: To stress that behavior can shape beliefs, not only the reverse
options.D: To explain why participants disliked being paid to lie

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说理论"反过来"是为了强调一个反直觉的观点：行为可以塑造信念，而不仅是信念决定行为。A是literal_restatement，只是复述实验设置，没解释"反过来"的用意。B是over_inference，教授没有说Festinger推翻了全部早期心理学。D是wrong_purpose，误把目的当成解释被试的情绪反应，与该句子无关。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how attitudes form?

options.A: Attitudes are fixed early and almost never change
options.B: Attitudes always result from careful rational thought
options.C: Attitudes are unaffected by a person's own behavior
options.D: Attitudes can shift to justify what a person has already done

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座结尾指出信念有时会悄悄重组来为已做的行为辩护，即态度可以为既成行为而转变。A是topic_adjacent，讲座并未说态度早早定型且几乎不变。B是reversed_logic，教授明确说态度改变常常不是理性过程。C是reversed_logic，整篇核心正是行为能影响态度，与此相反。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the role of patronage in shaping Baroque art
items.transcript: When we look at the dramatic ceilings and grand altarpieces of the Baroque period, we often credit the genius of individual artists. But that view misses something crucial: the patron. In the seventeenth century, almost no major work was created on speculation. It was commissioned. And the patron, whether a pope, a prince, or a wealthy religious order, shaped the work in ways we can still read in the finished painting. Consider the Catholic Church after the Council of Trent. The Church wanted art that would move ordinary worshippers emotionally and pull them back from Protestantism. So patrons commissioned scenes full of movement, dramatic light, and intense feeling. Caravaggio's paintings, with their stark contrasts of light and shadow, weren't just personal style. They answered a demand for immediacy and emotional power. Take Bernini, working for a series of popes in Rome. His sculptures and architecture were designed to overwhelm the viewer, to make the Church's authority feel magnificent and undeniable. The scale, the gilding, the theatrical staging, all of it served the patron's agenda. Now, this doesn't mean artists were mere employees with no voice. The best of them negotiated, pushed boundaries, and left a personal mark. But the subject matter, the size, even the emotional tone were often specified in advance by contract. So when you stand before a Baroque masterpiece, you're not only seeing an artist's vision. You're seeing a conversation, sometimes a tension, between what the artist wanted and what the patron required. Understanding who paid for the work, and why, tells us nearly as much about Baroque art as the brushwork itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The technical methods Caravaggio used to paint shadows
options.B: How patrons shaped the form and content of Baroque art
options.C: The theological decisions made at the Council of Trent
options.D: Why Baroque artists worked mainly in Rome and not elsewhere

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。讲座反复强调赞助人如何塑造巴洛克艺术的形式与内容，从教会需求到合同规定。A是too_narrow，卡拉瓦乔的明暗技法只是一个例子。C是wrong_topic，特伦特会议只是背景，并非讲座主旨。D是surface_word_trap，借用了"罗马"这个词，但讲座并未讨论艺术家为何集中在罗马。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Church want its commissioned art to do after the Council of Trent?

options.A: Display the personal styles of individual artists
options.B: Record historical events with strict accuracy
options.C: Move worshippers emotionally and counter Protestantism
options.D: Reduce the use of dramatic light and bright color

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座明确指出教会希望艺术在情感上打动普通信众，并把他们从新教拉回来。A是wrong_detail，讲座说画作回应需求而非展示个人风格。B是topic_adjacent，对历史精确记录的说法讲座从未提及。D是reversed_logic，教会恰恰要求充满动感和戏剧性光线，与此相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor discuss Bernini's work for the popes?

options.A: To restate that Bernini made both sculpture and architecture
options.B: To argue that popes were the only Baroque patrons
options.C: To show that artists ignored their patrons' wishes
options.D: To show how a patron's agenda shaped the work's scale and effect

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授用贝尼尼为教皇工作的例子说明赞助人的目的如何决定作品的规模和震撼效果。A是literal_restatement，只是复述贝尼尼做雕塑和建筑，没解释用意。B是over_inference，教授提到教皇、亲王、宗教团体多种赞助人，并未说只有教皇。C是wrong_purpose，与例子意图相反，例子正说明作品服务于赞助人议程。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the relationship between artist and patron?

options.A: The final work reflects both the artist's vision and the patron's demands
options.B: Artists had no influence over the works they produced
options.C: Patrons cared only about cost and never about content
options.D: Personal style was forbidden by most Baroque contracts

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授说最终作品是艺术家意愿与赞助人要求之间的"对话"甚至张力，二者都体现其中。B是over_generalization，讲座说最优秀者会谈判、留下个人印记，并非毫无影响。C是reversed_logic，赞助人恰恰高度关注题材和情感基调。D是topic_adjacent，合同常规定题材尺寸，但讲座没有说禁止个人风格。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.transcript: You probably think that if something obvious appears right in front of you, you'll notice it. But that assumption turns out to be surprisingly wrong. There's a phenomenon in cognitive psychology called inattentional blindness, and it means we can fail to see something fully visible simply because our attention is focused elsewhere. The key idea is that seeing isn't just about light hitting the retina. It's about where you direct your attention. The most famous demonstration comes from psychologists Daniel Simons and Christopher Chabris. They asked participants to watch a short video of people passing basketballs and to count how many passes one team made. While viewers were busy counting, a person in a gorilla suit walked right through the middle of the scene, stopped, thumped their chest, and walked off. Afterward, roughly half the participants reported they never saw the gorilla at all. Now, these weren't careless people. They were concentrating hard, which is exactly the point. Because their attention was locked onto counting passes, the unexpected gorilla simply didn't register in conscious awareness. This has real consequences outside the lab. Think about a driver focused on a conversation who fails to notice a cyclist, or a radiologist scanning for one type of abnormality who misses another. In each case, attention is doing its job of filtering, but that filtering has a cost. So the lesson is humbling. Our vivid sense that we perceive the whole world around us is partly an illusion. We see what we attend to, and a great deal of the visible world passes by unnoticed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the human retina processes incoming light
options.B: Why people enjoy watching basketball games
options.C: How focused attention can cause us to miss visible things
options.D: The training that radiologists receive to detect disease

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座主旨是无意视盲——当注意力集中别处时，我们会看不见眼前可见的东西。A是surface_word_trap，借用"视网膜"一词，但讲座明确说看见不只是光打到视网膜。B是wrong_topic，篮球只是实验素材，并非讲篮球。D是too_narrow，放射科医生只是结尾的一个应用例子。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what were participants asked to do in the Simons and Chabris study?

options.A: Identify a person wearing a gorilla suit on screen
options.B: Memorize the faces of the players in the video
options.C: Estimate how fast the basketball was moving
options.D: Count the number of passes made by one team

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座明确说参与者被要求数其中一队传了多少次球。A是reversed_logic，发现大猩猩恰恰是被试没被要求做、也大多没注意到的事。B是topic_adjacent，记忆球员面孔讲座从未提及。C是wrong_detail，任务是数传球次数，不是估算球速。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did about half the participants miss the gorilla?

options.A: Their attention was locked onto counting the passes
options.B: The gorilla moved too quickly to be seen clearly
options.C: The video quality made the figure hard to recognize
options.D: They had been told in advance to ignore distractions

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。讲座指出因为注意力锁定在数传球上，意外的大猩猩没进入意识。B是wrong_detail，大猩猩还停下捶胸，并非动得太快。C是topic_adjacent，视频清晰度问题讲座未提。D是wrong_detail，被试并未被告知要忽略干扰，他们只是专注于计数。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention a distracted driver and a radiologist?

options.A: To restate that the gorilla study used a video
options.B: To show that inattentional blindness has real-world consequences
options.C: To argue that drivers and doctors have unusually poor vision
options.D: To suggest that attention can never be trained or improved

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授举开车和放射科的例子，是为了说明无意视盲在实验室之外有真实后果。A是literal_restatement，与实验视频无关，是另一层用意。C是over_inference，例子说的是注意力过滤的代价，而非这些人视力差。D是over_inference，讲座并未断言注意力无法训练或改善。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how gift-giving rituals reinforce social bonds across cultures
items.transcript: In many cultures, a gift is never simply a gift. It carries an obligation, and that obligation is exactly what holds people together. Anthropologists call this reciprocity, the social rule that a gift received must, in some form, be returned. The French anthropologist Marcel Mauss explored this idea in his study of gift exchange in traditional societies. He argued that giving, receiving, and repaying form a cycle that binds individuals and groups into lasting relationships. Let me give you a vivid example: the kula exchange among the Trobriand Islanders of the Pacific. Islanders travel long distances by canoe to exchange shell ornaments. Necklaces move in one direction around a ring of islands, and armbands move in the opposite direction. Here's the striking part. These objects have no practical use. You can't wear them day to day, and they aren't sold. Their entire value lies in the act of exchange itself, in the bonds and the status it creates between trading partners. Notice that the timing matters too. A gift is rarely returned immediately. If you handed someone money the instant they gave you a present, it would feel like a cold transaction, not a relationship. The delay is what signals trust and keeps the partners connected over time. So gift-giving isn't really about the object changing hands. It's about creating a web of mutual obligation that ties a community together. Next time, we'll look at what happens when these expectations of reciprocity are broken, and how different cultures respond when someone fails to give back.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How shell ornaments are manufactured in the Pacific
options.B: Why Marcel Mauss traveled to the Trobriand Islands
options.C: How modern economies set prices for luxury goods
options.D: How gift-giving creates social bonds through reciprocity

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座主旨是赠礼如何通过互惠创造社会纽带，库拉交换是核心例证。A是too_narrow，贝壳饰品的制作讲座根本未谈。B是wrong_topic，讲座没说Mauss亲自去过特罗布里恩群岛。C是wrong_topic，现代经济如何定价与讲座主旨无关，反而强调这些物品不出售。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the kula exchange in detail?

options.A: To illustrate how exchange itself, not the object, creates social bonds
options.B: To explain how islanders earn a living by trading ornaments
options.C: To restate that necklaces and armbands move around a ring
options.D: To prove that shell ornaments are extremely valuable to wear

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授详述库拉交换，是为了说明价值在于交换行为本身而非物品，从而创造纽带。B是wrong_purpose，岛民并非靠交易这些物品谋生，它们不出售。C是literal_restatement，只复述项链臂环的流向，没解释用意。D是over_inference，讲座恰说这些饰品日常无法佩戴，并非证明其佩戴价值高。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out that a gift is rarely returned immediately?

options.A: To restate that the kula objects travel in two directions
options.B: To show that the delay signals trust and sustains the relationship
options.C: To argue that immediate repayment is physically impossible
options.D: To suggest that gifts should always be repaid with money

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授强调回礼不立即，是为了说明这种延迟传递信任、让关系持续。A是literal_restatement，复述物品流向，与延迟回礼无关。C是over_inference，讲座说立即回礼会显得冷冰冰，并非物理上不可能。D是wrong_purpose，教授恰用付钱的反例说明那样会变成冷交易，而非主张用钱回礼。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: The specific routes that canoes follow between islands
options.B: How shell ornaments are passed down within families
options.C: What happens when expectations of reciprocity are broken
options.D: Why Marcel Mauss chose to study traditional societies

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座结尾明确预告下次讨论当互惠期待被打破、不回礼时各文化如何反应。A是too_narrow，独木舟路线只是本次讲座的细节，不是下个主题。B是wrong_topic，饰品在家族内传承讲座从未提及。D是wrong_topic，研究Mauss为何选择传统社会与预告内容无关。
